caveat: NOTE: This is a statistical cross-check, not a replacement for interpretive coding. Topic models produce word clusters, not themes.

theme.name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

results.text: It's not about bringing in an AI technology. It's about doing exactly what you described, which is having a fundamental reckoning with What are we as an organization in a world where many of the assumptions we have are being tested and challenged by the broad-based adoption of AI in the world around us? What's our role in shepherding those core journalistic virtues in a world where attention is becoming fragmented, where people are finding fundamentally new ways of accessing and processing information? We're adversarial and bad actors are using AI in ways that are deeply kind of convoluted and manipulative. It requires an organizational reckoning and a potential for organizational visioning to ask who do we aspire to be in an AI-enabled world? And to me, that's a much more interesting conversation than whether we should pick vendor A or vendor B in order to do X task. Exactly. results.document: 2024-05-01 - Mattia Peretti Balancing AI Innovation with Journalistic Integrity.txt
results.text: It's not that everyone, every single person in a news organization needs to become an expert. in AI. I absolutely do not think that. We went through a few years ago in the journalism industry the time where everyone needed to become a coder and everyone needed to learn to code. I don't believe that's true. Definitely not with AI, but we do need to get that literacy because it's just having an impact on all of us as people in society, not just in journalism. And so we need to combine that everyone's understanding with the deeper expertise of someone in the organization, ideally not a single AI expert, but at the very least a team or a working group of people. So then those experiments that are run can immediately be understood by the newsroom so that the newsroom everyone can feed back and participate in whatever it is that an organization is trying to build. results.document: 2024-12-27 - Upasna Gautam The Product Manager's Guide to AI in News.txt
results.text: So like For example, are you a local newsroom trying to increase efficiency? Are you trying to improve content quality? Are you trying to enhance audience trust? Like a well-defined problem not only guides AI implementation, but it also ensures that those newsroom values and journalistic priorities and integrity remain intact. Along with asking the right questions to solve the right problems, you cannot solve every problem at once. And especially the smaller the newsroom is. Focus on the one big problem and break that problem down into smaller pieces. What is that North Star problem? You don't have the resources to try to be everywhere and everything for everyone. And I think there's a lot of great examples of local newsrooms who are doing this successfully. But I think this is again a great learning opportunity. And why I love the news product alliance is that we get to develop the resources and share the knowledge, you know, freely, literally and figuratively freely, so that we can e

results.document: 2026-01-27 - Melissa Bell, Aron Pilhofer, Mark Chonofsky & David Chivers Chicago Public Media on Building AI Tools That Serve the Audience.txt
results.text: Well, Melissa, I'd love to start with you to just compare where has the Chicago public media been from The time you started there with the Lenfest AI Collaborative Program, to where you've seen it, especially over the past year in 2025, how has it grown in its endeavors with AI. Sure. I started here a year and three months ago and when I joined there was some interest within the organization to experiment with AI. But as with many local and public media organizations, resources are tight. We're we don't have a lot of room for experimentation. We need to be very careful about how we're making our spend reach across editorial product maintenance, et cetera, et cetera. So there wasn't a lot of deep newsroom. revenue side, business side, product side, experimentation with AI. That of course puts us further behind the transformation that's happening around the world. And this project really allowed us to kick start our efforts to learn how we can apply this technology to our work. So from a

results.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
results.text: But also on their capacity to evaluate these new and incoming tools to ask questions about what they need to do to secure them. And then to intentionally create things like sandbox environments that are test beds where journalists can go out and try and play with these tools without creating the risk of new vulnerabilities. So that's kind of the phase two approach, and that's where organizations need to very quickly develop a new muscle and capacity to create ecosystems of AI exploration so that those innovators in the field can do what they need to. And there's a third phase to this work, which is, you know, if we started from okay, we're playing around with tools to just get to know them, we're now using the tools that exist to do what we want to do. Well, maybe the kind of like aspiring plateau here is to get to phase three. where we're actually saying let's stop being consumers of commercial AI tools and build the tools we need in order to do our work effectively. results.document: 2024-12-27 - Upasna Gautam The Product Manager's Guide to AI in News.txt
results.text: I'm sure we can get into that in a lot more detail. Going back to your other question is like how does the team structure at CNN work? It works very much like a tech company, and we're evolving more and more to adopt a lot of those similar organizational frameworks and structures. So our platform team is definitely like a core team. And then we have squads across these teams as well. So for example, the mobile team is a squad. The video and audio experience is a squad. We've got personalization. We've got machine learning data products. All of those are separate teams. The platform team, since we serve the internal customer, which is our journalists and our editors, we also function as consultants for those other squads because they're building features and products on our platform on Stellar. And so we work with them to validate requirements, to identify any gaps. to make sure we're on the same page about the problems that we're solving and to make sure that the execution is flawless.
